content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Asistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660              322-5901            322-6441                322-6444

August 2, 1983


Honorable Diane E. Watson
California State Senate
State Capitol, Room 4040
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our No. A-83-158

Dear Senator Watson:

     You have asked what reporting obligations, if any, you would
have under the Political Reform Act if you were to create an
independent, discretionary fund in your name to benefit various
non-profit and non-political organizations in your district.
Funds would be raised through contributions and fundraisers, and
would be expended in direct payments to these organizations and
to you for expenses, including travel and lodging, related to
your personal appearances for the Fund.  You suggested two
possible methods of establishing such a fund: establishment of
an independent, non-profit corporation, or establishment of a
separate, distinct bank account in your name.

     Whether or not the fund has full reporting obligations under
the Act will depend on whether donations to the fund are
considered to be contributions.  If they are, the fund will be a
controlled recipient committee under Government Code Sections
82013 and 82016, and will have to report in the same manner as
any other controlled recipient committee.1/

_______________
     1/ It is common for political committees, as well as for
other types of organizations, to contribute to non-profit
community groups.  If you want advice as to whether a political
committee can expend most or all of its funds on such
contributions, you should ask the Attorney General for advice
under Elections Code Sections 12401, et seq.

Honorable Diane E. Watson
August 2, 1983
Page 2

A contribution is defined in Government Code Section 82015:

"Contribution" means a payment, . . . except to
the extent that full and adequate consideration is
received unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes....

According to Commission regulations, donations to a fund
will be considered contributions, if the donations are made for
political purposes, or if the donations are used by the fund to
make payments which are either contributions or political
expenditures under the Act. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215
provides:

(a) A contribution is any monetary or nonmonetary
payment made for political purposes for which full and
adequate consideration is not made to the donor.

. . .

(c) "Contribution" includes any monetary or
nonmonetary payment for which full and adequate
consideration is not made to the donor that is received
by any person or organization other than a candidate,
controlled committee, official committee of a political
party, or organization formed or existing primarily for
political purposes if the payment is "earmarked" for
the making of contributions or expenditures. A payment
is "earmarked" when, at the time of making the payment,
the donor knows or has reason to know that the payment
or funds with which the payment will be commingled will
be used to make contributions, as defined in Government
Code Section 82015 and this regulation, or
expenditures, as defined in Government Code Section
82025 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225....

In order to determine whether or not payments by the fund
are contributions or expenditures, it is necessary to look both
at the purpose of the payments and at the possible political
benefit to you. Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18215 provides that a payment is a contribution if it is:

(a) . . .

(1) For the purpose of influencing or
attempting to influence the action of the voters

Honorable Diane E. Watson
August 2, 1983
Page 3


     for or against the nomination or election of a
candidate or candidates, or the qualification or
passage of any measure; or

     (2) Received by or made at the behest of:

          (A) A candidate, unless it is clear
from surrounding circumstances that the
payment was received or made at his or her
behest for personal purposes unrelated to his
or her candidacy or status as an office
holder....

          (b) "Made at the behest" means a payment made
under the control or at the direction of a
candidate....

     The definition of an expenditure is similar to that of a
contribution. Section 82025 provides:

     "Expenditure" means a payment, a forgiveness of a
loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an
enforceable promise to make a payment, unless it is
clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not
made for political purposes....

     Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225 further
defines the term expenditure:

     (a) An expenditure is any monetary or nonmonetary
payment made for political purposes for which full and
adequate consideration is not made to the donor. A
payment is made for political purposes if it is:

          (1) For the purpose of influencing or
attempting to influence the action of the voters
for or against the nomination or election of a
candidate or candidates, or the qualification or
passage of any measure; or

          (2) Made by:

               (A) A candidate, unless it is clear
from surrounding circumstances that the
payment was made for personal purposes
unrelated to his or her candidacy or status
as an office holder;

Honorable Diane E. Watson
August 2, 1983
Page 4


. . .

(c)  Any payment used to make contributions, as
defined in Government Code Section 82015 and 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18215, is an expenditure.

On the basis of these definitions of expenditures and
contributions, donations to the fund will almost certainly be
contributions, and the fund will be a recipient committee, if
payments made by the fund are either:

(1)  made for the purpose of influencing voters; or

(2)  received by you, controlled by you, or made at
your direction, and it is not clear from the surrounding
circumstances that the payments are for personal purposes
unrelated to your candidacy or status as an officeholder.

We cannot tell you in advance whether any proposed payment
by the fund will or will not be considered to be for political
purposes.  However, as a general rule, if you control, direct
and receive payments from the fund, and the fund makes payments
using your name, either you will be deemed to have received
contributions, or the payments will be considered to be
expenditures, or both, unless you can show that you received no
political benefit from the payments.  Whatever we might conclude
with respect to individual future payments, I can foresee that
questions as to the payments will be raised, particularly as to
payments for your appearances, or for activities or donations
made in your name in your district during your campaign.  Since
it seems likely that at least some of the fund's payments will
be of political benefit to you, and thus be either contributions
or expenditures, it is likely that donations to the fund will
be contributions, and that the fund will be treated as a recipient
committee.

In your letter, you asked whether a different result might
be reached if the fund was controlled by a non-profit
corporation.  Again, the question is one of your personal
involvement in the corporation.  If you control or direct the
expenditures by the corporation, the situation will be the same
as with the unincorporated fund.  If, on the other hand, the
corporation is truly independent, and you neither direct nor
control its activities, it is possible that payments by the fund
will not be treated as contributions or expenditures.  The legal
result will depend on the specific nature of your relationship
to the corporation and on the specific circumstances of the
corporation's activities.

Honorable Diane E. Watson
August 2, 1983
Page 5


     I hope this letter has been helpful to you.  I am sorry that
we cannot give a more definitive answer to your questions.
However, I hope I have made it clear that there is a good chance
that any discretionary fund that you establish which will
receive donations, and will make payments to public interest
organizations in your name or at your behest in your district,
is likely to be considered a recipient committee under the
Political Reform Act.

     You also asked about use of the State Seal or legislative
letterheads.  Questions about the State Seal should be addressed
to the Secretary of State.  Questions about legislative
letterheads or insignia should be addressed to Legislative
Counsel.  If you have any further questions concerning the
Political Reform Act, please do not hesitate to call me.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:plh

SACRAMENTO OFFICE
STATE CAPITOL, ROOM 4040
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
(916) 445-5215

RICK ROGERS
ADMINISTRATIVE ASSISTANT

DISTRICT OFFICE
4401 CRENSHAW BLVD.
SUITE 201
LOS ANGELES, CA 90043
(213) 291-6665

BILL HOSTON
ADMINISTRATIVE ASSISTANT

COMMITTEES
CHAIRPERSON
HEALTH AND WELFARE
AND SUBCOMMITTEE
LONG TERM
CARE SERVICES

California State Senate
JUL 5 6 59 AM '03
DIANE E. WATSON

JOINT COMMITTEE ON
PUBLIC TRUST

TWENTY-EIGHTH SENATORIAL DISTRICT

CHAIRPERSON
CALIFORNIA LEGISLATIVE
BLACK CAUCUS

CHAIRPERSON

INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS

Senate Committee on Health and Welfare

BUSINESS AND
PROFESSIONS

EDUCATION

JUDICIARY

Ms. Barbara Milman, Legal Division Chief
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA., 95804

Dear Ms. Milman,

As per our phone inquiry of June 30, I am requesting a legal
opinion on the possibility of creating an independent,
discretionary fund in my name. I have outlined below two
different structures and uses for such a fund. I would like your
opinion and advice on creating this vehicle. If you could
include both legal issues and practical considerations based on
your experience, I would be appreciative.

(1) Establishment of an Independent Non-profit Corporation.
I would create and duly register a non-profit corporation, to
be administered by an executive committee according to accepted
by-laws. Funds would be collected, and dispersed at the
discretion of the committee. It is expected that these funds
would be used to benefit various non-profit and non-political
organizations in the 28th Senate District and North-Central Los
Angeles. These would include citizen's and church groups, health
related organizations, victims beneficial groups, and similar
causes. The money would be available to cover expenses related to
my own efforts on behalf of these organizations, and would cover
the costs of my transportation and lodging while working for
these groups or advocacy undertaken for them.

(2) Establishment of an Independent Discretionary Fund.
This option would simply involve the establishment of a
distinct and independent fund, in my name, administered by a
single individual. This fund would be financed through sporting
events, personal appearances and contributions. The fund would
be used to pay expenses incurred in my work for various community
organizations. There would be no direct, personal payments to
myself nor any benefit to me beyond those allowing me wider
involvement with non-profit groups in my district or statewide.

I would like to further inquire whether or not there would be
any breach of law in using the seal or any other symbol of my
office relating to either of these options. If you need any
further information in providing this opinion, please contact
John Miller of my staff.

I appreciate your assistance in this matter.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Diane P. Watson
Senator, 28th Dist.

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

July 6, 1983

Honorable Diane E. Watson
State Senator
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: A-83-158

Dear Senator Watson:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Robert E. Leidigh, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Barbara A. Milman
Chief, Legal Division

sections.conclusion: It is likely that donations to the fund will be contributions, and that the fund will be treated as a recipient committee. If you control or direct the expenditures by the corporation, the situation will be the same as with the unincorporated fund. If, on the other hand, the corporation is truly independent, and you neither direct nor control its activities, it is possible that payments by the fund will not be treated as contributions or expenditures. sections.question_synthetic: Senator Watson asks whether a discretionary charitable fund established in her name to benefit non-profit organizations in her district would be subject to Political Reform Act reporting requirements, and whether the form of the fund (independent non-profit corporation vs. separate bank account) would affect this determination.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that donations to any discretionary fund Senator Watson establishes that receives donations and makes payments in her name or at her behest will likely be treated as contributions and the fund as a recipient committee with full reporting obligations. A truly independent non-profit corporation not controlled by Senator Watson might avoid this classification, but the outcome depends on the actual nature of her relationship and involvement with the corporation.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Clear FPPC response with identifiable Question and Conclusion sections. Document includes OCR artifacts and a second partial letter at end (follow-up correspondence). Main advice letter is complete and analyzable. Minor OCR degradation in footer area does not significantly impact extraction.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises Senator Diane E. Watson that a discretionary charitable fund established in her name to benefit non-profit organizations in her district would likely be treated as a recipient committee under the Political Reform Act with full reporting obligations, unless the fund is structured as a truly independent entity over which she exercises no control.
